does: This paper investigates whether reward models that excel at inference-time guidance can effectively improve large language model reasoning during reinforcement learning training. The authors conduct systematic experiments comparing Outcome-supervised Reward Models (ORMs) and Process-supervised Reward Models (PRMs) combined with sparse success rewards across multiple LLM-based math problem solvers. They find that despite strong inference-time performance, these learned reward models frequently fail to improve or actively degrade RL training outcomes compared to using only sparse success signals, revealing a significant gap between inference-time utility and training-time effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature around reward modeling for LLM reasoning, extending prior research that demonstrated reward models' value for search-based inference in domains like mathematical problem-solving. It responds to existing frameworks like ORM and PRM that showed promise for post-hoc guidance, and connects to broader RL training approaches using dense versus sparse rewards. The paper engages with recent developments in process supervision and outcome supervision paradigms, while also tapping into fundamental questions about reward signal quality in RL that trace back to classical reinforcement learning theory regarding exploration, exploitation, and credit assignment.
enables: This work opens investigations into why the reward signal properties that matter for inference-time guidance differ fundamentally from those needed for effective RL training. It points toward developing training-specific reward models that properly shape the optimization landscape during RL, rather than simply repurposing inference-time models. The findings suggest future research on reward model design principles tailored for training, methods to diagnose when rewards help or hurt learning, and potentially hybrid approaches that adapt reward signals across training phases. This could lead to better understanding of how to construct reward landscapes that genuinely guide policy optimization rather than mislead it.
